description:
A fortified Bureau frontier post at the edge of settled country. Adjutant Renna Vask runs Harrowgate station with too few hands, too many raids, and a commission that barely stretches to cover the road. She'll hear Halden out — but a place on the roster has to be earned. The proving contract sends him out onto the road to clear a reported raid: a real fight, no hand-holding. Edric Voss, a Bureau regular, is assigned to accompany him.

Triggers:
1. On encounter start (vask_station_aigm — aigm), the AIGM opens with Vask sizing Halden up across her desk — overworked, dry, not unkind — and laying out the situation.
2. When the player enters Vask's desk region (vask_speech — speech), Vask addresses Halden directly with the terms of the proving contract and names Voss as his assigned companion.
3. When the player enters Vask's desk region (vask_talked_flag — set_flag), the flag "vask_talked" is set to signal that the briefing is complete.
4. When "vask_talked" is set (edric_joins — aigm), the AIGM narrates Edric Voss stepping forward and falling into step with Halden — he is now a companion.
5. When the player crosses into the proving-ground zone (proving_contract_announcement — announcement), a full-screen beat signals the shift from the station to the field.
6. The raiding party (ambush_combat — combat) springs the ambush when Halden enters the kill zone on the road.
7. On encounter completion (debrief_aigm — aigm), the AIGM runs Vask's debrief: Hired Blade rank, Standing explained, the coordination note planted.
customIntroduction:
The road into Harrowgate is older than the post that guards it. You can see the Bureau station from a quarter-mile out — a low fortified building with a muster yard behind a timber gate, Bureau colours hung from a bracket above the door, one regular on watch who looks like he hasn't slept since the last raid came in. The town behind it is small and tired. The Reach stretches out in every direction, too much of it and not enough people to hold it.

You've come looking for honest work. The Bureau is the only institution in the Reach that pays for blades without asking too many questions about where they came from — as long as you can prove you're worth the contract.
customContext:
COMBAT-FORWARD, NO TUTORIAL FRAMING.

The player character is Halden Marrick — a young Dwarf Fighter, soldier background, fresh off the mountain and hungry for honest work and a name. This is Part One of a structured adventure set in the Sundered Reach.

SETTING TONE: The Bureau of Adjustment holds this stretch of frontier with chronic understrength. The station at Harrowgate is real and unglamorous — chipped stone, a single flagpole, a muster roll that's never full. The work is honest and the pay is honest and neither is generous.

ADJUTANT RENNA VASK (neutral NPC, on roster): Dry, fair, overworked. She has seen a hundred blades walk through that gate and she is not easily impressed. She reads Halden in about thirty seconds — another mountain fighter chasing a name — and she is not dismissive about it, just accurate. She explains the situation plainly: the Bureau has an imperial commission to hire blades against raiding bandits, goblins, and stray skeletons the White Capes won't fight (not their jurisdiction; the Capes hunt magic, not rabble). A place on the roster isn't given — it's earned. She sends Halden out on a PROVING CONTRACT: clear a reported raid on the east road.

THE PROVING CONTRACT FIGHT: Run it as a real level-1 encounter — dangerous enough to matter. The enemy group is a small raiding band (bandits, goblins, or skeletons — pick or roll at table). No slow-walking the basics. Note how Halden handles it: does he charge, hold a chokepoint, try to take a prisoner? Vask will review what comes back.

ON RETURN — DEBRIEF: Vask signs Halden onto the commission at starting rank HIRED BLADE and explains the Standing system: contracts are posted on the board, pay is per head and per job, the Bureau remembers who delivers and who runs. Standing starts accumulating now.

She also notes, with the flat affect of someone reporting a weather pattern she doesn't like: the raids have been too coordinated lately. Different families hitting the same roads on the same nights. That's not the usual rabble. She doesn't have a theory yet. She files the observation and moves on. (This thread pays off in the capstone.)

BEAT ORDER:
1. Arrival at Harrowgate station — Vask's office, the pitch, the terms.
2. The proving contract — field fight, real stakes.
3. Return debrief — sign-on, Hired Blade rank, Standing explained, the coordination note planted.

END STATE: The roster is open. Part Two sandbox begins.
objective: Survive the proving contract. Return to Adjutant Vask and earn a place on the Bureau roster.

then.text: You — yes, you. If you're here about the roster, come talk to me. I've got work that needs doing.
Edric Voss. Bureau regular — I've run this road before. Stay on my flank and we'll have those two sorted before dark.

then.message: Edric Voss — who just named himself — pushes off the wall where he has been waiting, pulls on his coat, and falls into step beside Halden. No more ceremony than that: a nod, the manner of a man who saves conversation for after the work. He is now a companion for the proving contract. Do not re-introduce him; he has already given his name.

Both raiders are down and the road has gone quiet. Give a short beat — Voss confirming the kill, the field going still — then point Halden back toward the station: Vask will want to hear it from him before anything's official. Do NOT narrate the sign-on or name a rank yet — that happens when Halden reports back to Vask.

The player is back at Harrowgate station between contracts. Vask is at her desk; the contract board is waiting. Do NOT replay the sign-on backstory — the player has been here before. A short beat that the player has returned to take work, then wait; Vask won't start the offer herself.

The player has just returned from a finished contract. Vask looks up from the ledger — she's been expecting them. Narrate her acknowledging the return with her dry, plain affect ("You came back. Show me what you've got.") and then defer to the conversation system for the turn-in. Do not name the reward yourself.
Vask has just paid the contract out. A clean beat — coin counted onto the desk, the entry inked into the ledger. The player can ask for another contract or step away.
